title: Growing-Season Management & Harvest
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

required.title: [Book] The Organic Gardener's Handbook of Natural Pest and Disease Control
required.author: edited by Fern Marshall Bradley, Barbara W. Ellis, and Deborah L. Martin

competence_markers.description: The student can identify the five most common garden pests in their region and describe ecological management strategies for each
The student uses physical barriers (row cover, etc.) as a primary pest management tool
The student has built and managed at least one season extension structure
The student can explain efficient irrigation principles and practices watering at root level
The student harvests crops at appropriate maturity and can explain harvest timing for at least five crops
The student maintains an IPM scouting log and harvest record through a full season

content.why_this_matters:
Planning a garden is one thing. Managing it through a real growing season — with pests, weather, disease, irrigation failures, and the relentless pressure of weeds — is another. This packet covers the operational skills that determine whether a well-planned garden actually produces food: integrated pest management, season extension, irrigation, and the decision-making that happens daily from May through October.

The best garden plan in the world fails without competent daily management. The person who can walk through a garden, diagnose problems from observation, and respond with the right intervention at the right time — that person produces food. The person who planned beautifully but cannot troubleshoot a wilting tomato does not.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Integrated pest management (IPM)** — ecological pest control strategies before any spray, including physical barriers, beneficial insects, companion planting, and hand management
2. **Season extension** — cold frames, row covers, low tunnels, and thermal management to double the productive season
3. **Irrigation management** — efficient water delivery, mulching for moisture retention, reading water stress
4. **Fertilization timing** — matching crop nutrition to growth stage
5. **Harvest timing and technique** — knowing when crops are at peak and harvesting efficiently
6. **Problem diagnosis** — reading plant symptoms and responding to root causes
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Integrated Pest Management

Pest management in a regenerative garden starts with ecology, not sprays. The hierarchy:

**1. Prevention (best):** Healthy soil produces healthy plants that resist pests and disease. Crop rotation prevents pest population buildup. Proper spacing allows air circulation that prevents fungal disease. Removing crop debris at season's end eliminates overwintering sites.

**2. Physical barriers:** Row cover draped over hoops excludes cabbage moths, flea beetles, cucumber beetles, and most flying insects. Copper tape around beds deters slugs. Collars around seedling stems prevent cutworm damage. These are the most effective, lowest-impact pest management tools available.

**3. Beneficial insect habitat:** Plant flowers in and around the garden. Alyssum, dill, fennel, yarrow, and native wildflowers attract parasitic wasps, ladybugs, lacewings, and hoverflies — predators that eat the pests you are trying to manage. A garden surrounded by bare ground or lawn has no beneficial insect reservoir.

**4. Companion planting (evidence-based only):** Some companion planting claims are folk wisdom without basis. Focus on what works: basil near tomatoes deters certain moths. Marigolds suppress root-knot nematodes (this is well-documented). Interplanting aromatic herbs among vegetables provides general pest confusion.

**5. Hand removal:** For large pests — tomato hornworms, squash bugs, cabbage worms — hand-pick them into soapy water. This is tedious but effective for small gardens and teaches the student to observe closely.

**6. Biological and organic sprays (last resort):** Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) for caterpillar pests (cabbage worms, tomato hornworms). Neem oil for soft-bodied insects and as a fungicide. Kaolin clay (Surround) as a physical barrier spray. These are organic and targeted, but they are still interventions — used only when the above methods are insufficient.

**The key principle:** Most pest outbreaks are symptoms of imbalance. A garden with healthy soil, diverse plantings, beneficial insect habitat, and proper management will have fewer pest problems than a monoculture strip with bare soil and no flowers. Fix the system first.

### Part 2: Season Extension

Most gardeners accept that the growing season runs from last frost to first frost. With basic season extension, you can add 4–8 weeks on each end — potentially doubling the months of fresh production.

**Row cover:** Lightweight floating row cover (0.5 oz/sq yd) provides 2–4 degrees F of frost protection while transmitting 85% of light and 100% of water. Draped over hoops, it creates a mini-greenhouse effect. Use for spring crops to get them in the ground 2–3 weeks before last frost and for fall crops to extend them 3–4 weeks past first frost.

**Cold frames:** A bottomless box with a transparent lid (glass or polycarbonate) set over a garden bed. Captures solar heat during the day, retains it at night. Can extend the season by 6–8 weeks. Management challenge: ventilation. On a sunny day, even in winter, a closed cold frame can overheat to 100+ degrees F and cook your plants. Prop the lid open on sunny days, close it before evening.

**Low tunnels:** Wire or PVC hoops over a bed, covered with greenhouse plastic or heavy row cover. Larger than a cold frame, same principle. More space for taller crops. Same ventilation challenge.

**Thermal mass:** Dark-colored water jugs inside a cold frame or tunnel absorb solar heat during the day and release it at night, moderating temperature swings. Simple, effective, and free.

**Fall and winter crops:** Many crops thrive in cool conditions: spinach, kale, chard, lettuce, arugula, carrots, beets, radishes, turnips, leeks. With season extension, these can be harvested into December and beyond in most climates. The key: plant them in late summer so they reach near-maturity before daylight drops below 10 hours. Growth slows dramatically in short days, but established plants survive cold temperatures that would kill newly planted ones.

### Part 3: Irrigation Management

Water is the most common limiting factor in garden production. Too little and plants wilt, stress, and produce poorly. Too much and roots drown, diseases flourish, and nutrients leach.

**Mulching:** The first and best irrigation management tool. Three to four inches of straw, leaves, or wood chips over the soil surface reduces evaporation by 50–70%. Mulched beds need watering half as often as bare beds.

**Drip irrigation:** The most efficient method for row crops. Delivers water directly to the root zone with minimal evaporation. A basic drip system costs $50–100 for a small garden and saves hours of watering time. Run drip lines under mulch for maximum efficiency.

**Watering principles:**
- Water deeply and infrequently rather than shallowly and often. Deep watering encourages deep root growth. Shallow watering produces shallow-rooted plants that wilt at the first dry spell.
- Water at the root zone, not overhead. Overhead watering wastes water to evaporation and promotes foliar disease.
- Water in the morning when possible. Evening watering leaves foliage wet overnight, inviting fungal disease.

**Reading water stress:** Wilting in the afternoon heat is normal for some crops (squash, cucumbers) and does not necessarily mean they need water — check the soil. Wilting in the morning means the plant is genuinely water-stressed. Leaf curl, dull color, and slow growth are chronic water stress indicators.

### Part 4: Harvest Timing and Technique

Every crop has an optimal harvest window. Picking too early wastes yield. Picking too late sacrifices quality.

**General principles:**
- Harvest in the morning when produce is coolest and most hydrated
- Use a sharp knife or pruners for clean cuts — tearing damages the plant
- Harvest regularly — many crops (beans, squash, cucumbers) produce more when picked frequently; unharvested fruit signals the plant to stop producing
- Handle produce gently to avoid bruising, which accelerates decay

**Specific timing:**
- Tomatoes: fully colored on the vine for best flavor; can be picked at first color change and ripened indoors if frost threatens
- Peppers: green is immature but edible; full color (red, orange, yellow) is ripe and sweeter
- Leafy greens: harvest outer leaves when full-sized, leaving the center to keep growing (cut-and-come-again method)
- Root crops: pull one to check size; harvest when roots have reached usable size but before they get woody
- Winter squash: when the stem begins to dry and the skin resists denting with a fingernail; cure in the sun for 7–10 days before storage

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. IPM Practice
**Developing well:** The student scouts regularly, identifies pests accurately, and responds with ecological strategies before reaching for sprays. They can explain why they chose a particular intervention.

**Developing slowly:** The student either ignores pests until damage is severe or immediately asks "what do I spray?" without considering ecological alternatives.

### 2. Season Extension
**Developing well:** The student has set up and managed a season extension structure, monitored temperatures, and produced food outside the normal frost-free window.

**Developing slowly:** The structure was built but not managed — killed plants through overheating or was abandoned before producing results.

### 3. Harvest Documentation
**Developing well:** Weekly harvest records with weights or counts by crop. End-of-season analysis identifying top producers and underperformers with proposed adjustments.

**Developing slowly:** Sporadic records. No end-of-season analysis.
